front: Practice: Islamic practice of ritualized "remembrance," recited or chanted (often the 99 names of God), sometimes counted on prayer beads
Practice: the whirling dance of the Mevlevi order is itself considered a form of this practice
Practice: the Naqshbandi order practices a distinctive silent form of this practice, sometimes synchronizing invocations of God's names with the heartbeat
Practice: per Surah Ar-Ra'd, adherents' hearts "find peace" through this practice of chanting du'a and Allah's many names
Practice: institutionalized group performance of this practice through the sama ceremony has been derided by critics as bid'ah ("innovation")
Practice: this ceremony often features the ney flute and tambourines
work: Sama and the Mevlevi Whirling Ceremony

front: Practice: an improvisatory flute solo called a taksim is part of this ceremony
work: Sama and the Mevlevi Whirling Ceremony

front: Practice: whirling only occurs during this section of the Sama ceremony, named for the number of times believers welcome Allah during the spiritual journey
work: Sama and the Mevlevi Whirling Ceremony

front: Practice: some adherents use erotic poetry during this ceremony to attain wajd, or ecstasy
work: Sama and the Mevlevi Whirling Ceremony

front: Practice: ceremonial garb for this ritual includes the white frock tannur, the long-sleeved destegul, and a tall pill-shaped cap called a sikke
work: Sama and the Mevlevi Whirling Ceremony

front: Practice: different Sufi branches are represented by the different colors of the tanoura garment worn during this ceremony
work: Sama and the Mevlevi Whirling Ceremony

front: Denomination: known in the West as the "Whirling Dervishes," this order was founded by followers of the poet Rumi
work: Sama and the Mevlevi Whirling Ceremony

front: Practice: Nusrat Fateh Ali Khan, nicknamed the "Shahenshah" ("King of Kings"), performed the vocals of this South Asian devotional genre
Practice: this genre's best-known performer collaborated with Michael Brook on the crossover album Mustt Mustt
Practice: musicians of this genre contributed to Peter Gabriel's soundtrack for The Last Temptation of Christ
Practice: a Massive Attack remix of the song "Mustt Mustt" became the first Urdu song to chart in the UK
Practice: the Sabri Brothers achieved fame performing this South Asian devotional genre, often played at urs (death-anniversary) ceremonies
Practice: this South Asian devotional song genre traces its origins to the Chishti Sufi order
Practice: Moroccan Sufi ensembles play instruments such as the lira and gimbri
Practice: Moroccan Sufi music strongly influenced this North African musical genre
Practice: a study by musicologist Richard C. Jankowsky examined a Sufi music tradition's "ambient" character, tracing contributions from women, Jews, and laborers
Denomination: disciples called muridun follow a spiritual guide called a murshid within these organizations
Practice: these organizations trace teaching lineages back to Muhammad (or, for one order, to Abu Bakr) through this kind of chain of teachers
Practice: new members of Sufi orders may receive this type of ceremonial cloak
Practice: Sufi orders gather at lodges known by regional names such as tekke or zawiya
Religion: major orders (turuq) within this tradition include the Naqshbandi, Qadiriyya, Chishti, Bektashi, Mevlevi, Tijaniyya, and Senussi
Denomination: unlike other Sufi orders, this order traces its teaching lineage through the first Caliph Abu Bakr rather than Ali
Denomination: this order is named for Baha' al-Din Naqshband, who came from the city of Bukhara
Denomination: an offshoot of this order arose from a debate between "unity of witness" and "unity of being," founded by Ahmad Sirhindi and spread to Iraq by Khalid al-Baghdadi
Denomination: this order was brought to India by a Sufi saint buried at a famous dargah in Ajmer
Denomination: the musician Amir Khusrau belonged to this Sufi order
Denomination: Akbar the Great was a patron of this Sufi order
Denomination: this order's member Salim Chishti, mentor to Akbar's son, was rewarded with a marble tomb that became the center of Fatehpur Sikri
Denomination: this order, founded by Ahmad al-Tijani, was one of the first Sufi orders to require exclusive membership
Denomination: this North African order descends from Idris I
Denomination: scholar Fazlur Rahman theorized a more politically active "neo-" version of these organizations arising during the 18th-19th centuries, at the cost of their metaphysical character
Figure: wrote the Masnavi, or "Spiritual Couplets," which inspired the founding of the Mevlevi order
Figure: opened a major work with "The Song of the Reed Flute"
Figure: had a close relationship with his spiritual teacher Shams-i Tabrizi (Shams of Tabriz)
Figure: collected ninety ghazals dedicated to his teacher in a work called the Divan
Figure: a collection of this poet's discourses is titled "It Is What It Is"
Figure: translator Coleman Barks, who neither reads nor speaks Persian, has been criticized for erasing Islamic elements from this poet's work in The Essential Rumi
Figure: is the subject of the novel The Forty Rules of Love, in which every chapter starts with the letter "B"
Work: this collection of Ibn Arabi's writings is also known as the Bezels of Wisdom
Work: also called the Illuminations, this heavily Qur'an-focused work by Ibn Arabi devotes much attention to God's "engendering command," or amr, to "Be!"
Figure: called ash-Shaykh al-Akbar ("the Greatest Master") despite not considering himself part of Sufism
Doctrine: this concept of the "Perfect Man," developed by Ibn Arabi, is compared to a "polished mirror"
Doctrine: Ibn Arabi held that his "Perfect Man" understands this concept, the "unity of being"
Figure: held that tawhid, not existence, is the ultimate truth underlying all other truths in his philosophy
Doctrine: Ibn Arabi used this concept to describe how things simultaneously unite and divide
Work: this Ibn Arabi collection gathers erotic poems addressed to doves
Figure: scholar Toshihiko Izutsu linked this philosopher's "perfect man" concept to the sages of Daoism in a comparative study
Figure: wrote the Revival of the Religious Sciences (Ihya), which defended Sufism as inspired by the Quran and made it acceptable within orthodox Sunni Islam
Work: this treatise, a critique of the Aristotelian falsafa tradition of al-Farabi, Avicenna, and Averroes, was later rebutted by Averroes' The Incoherence of the Incoherence
Work: this commentary on the Quran's Light Verse discusses three types of light and concludes God is the only true light
Figure: promoted occasionalism in a treatise attacking Aristotelian philosophy
Figure: this theologian's writings influenced Usman dan Fodio's treatise supporting the teaching of religious sciences in the Sokoto Caliphate
Doctrine: this concept of ego-annihilation is summarized as "dying before one dies"
Doctrine: this state of "perpetual existence" follows fana, and involves a three-graded alignment with the divine plan
Doctrine: this ultimate divine unity is the goal most associated with Rumi's poetry and the sama ceremony
Doctrine: advanced practitioners train to unlock a group of supersensory organs known as "The Six Subtleties"
Doctrine: this quality, translated as "certainty," is a form of gnosis with graded degrees of mystical knowing
Doctrine: adherents originated this concept of the "Perfected Man" who heads a hierarchy of saints upholding world order
Religion: members of this tradition are sometimes referred to by a term that literally translates to "desirous ones"
Denomination: this West African Islamic state was founded by Usman dan Fodio, who took the Fulani people on jihad and conquered Hausaland
work: West African Sufism: Sokoto Caliphate and the Muridiyya

front: Denomination: Usman dan Fodio and his daughter Nana Asma'u belonged to this Sufi order
work: West African Sufism: Sokoto Caliphate and the Muridiyya

front: Figure: this scholar-poet, daughter of Usman dan Fodio, founded the Yan Taru movement of women's Islamic educators
work: West African Sufism: Sokoto Caliphate and the Muridiyya

front: Figure: this poet wrote a poem in praise of Sufi women that mentions the sainthood of Rabi'a Basri
work: West African Sufism: Sokoto Caliphate and the Muridiyya

front: Denomination: this brotherhood, based in Touba, Senegal, was founded by/commemorates Amadou Bamba via its massive annual Grand Magal pilgrimage
work: West African Sufism: Sokoto Caliphate and the Muridiyya

front: Holiday: during this pilgrimage to Touba, adherents drink coffee flavored with peppery seeds called djar
work: West African Sufism: Sokoto Caliphate and the Muridiyya

front: Denomination: this brotherhood holds de facto political rule over Touba, Senegal, and was noted by pan-Africanist theorist Cheikh Anta Diop
work: West African Sufism: Sokoto Caliphate and the Muridiyya

front: Denomination: the Muridiyya and Tijaniyya are cited as African-based examples of these Sufi organizations
work: West African Sufism: Sokoto Caliphate and the Muridiyya

front: Practice: adherents commonly visit the tombs of favored Sufi saints; Muhammad's own tomb is marked by a green dome
Practice: wandering West/North African Islamic teachers venerated at pilgrimage tomb-sites, possessing high levels of baraka, are known by this term (from a root meaning "garrison")
Practice: these teachers originally lived in monastic fortresses called ribats; a "Grand" one leads the Mouride brotherhood
Religion: the preponderance of mausoleums belonging to this tradition's figures earned the Pakistani cities of Uch and Multan the nickname "City of Saints"
Holiday: this carnival-esque holiday commemorating Muhammad's birth was popularized by Sufism
Holiday: Ahmadiyya and Wahhabi communities reject this holiday as bid'ah ("innovation")
Practice: Salafi critics accuse Sufis of this sin, roughly translated as "polytheism"
Practice: in Harer, Ethiopia, Sufis chew this stimulant drug to facilitate spiritual experiences and heighten their sense of union with Allah
Holiday: Sufis in Harer commemorate this holiday by feeding hyenas porridge for prosperity
Practice: this 40-day retreat of fasting and praying in a dark space is most commonly performed within Sufism
Religion: possibly derives its name from an Arabic root meaning "those who wear wool," reflecting historical depictions of its adherents' clothing
Religion: spread widely along Central Asian trade routes, often accompanying merchant caravans
Religion: early Western scholarship on this tradition was popularized by Ivan Agueli and Rene Guenon
Religion: al-Muhasibi and his disciple Junayd are cited as early figures in this tradition
Religion: practitioners are also called by this term, originally used to describe mendicant dervishes
Religion: this tradition was introduced to Australia by Friedrich von Frankenberg, whose student Francis Brabazon built the "Avatar's Abode" to house the leader of its "Reoriented" offshoot
Doctrine: Inayat Khan, founder of the International Sufi Movement, centered his belief on the unity of God with all things, referring to it by this term
Religion: Chinese practitioners construct gongbei memorials on the graves of menhuan lineage founders, such as the early modern leader Ma Laichi, whose shrine is in Linxia
Religion: the Safavid dynasty's originally Kurdish ruling family rose to prominence as leaders of tariqas within this tradition, before Ismail I established Shi'a Islam as Iran's state religion
Place: the Darqawa Sufi order led a 19th-century revival of ascetic practice in this country, which Clifford Geertz contrasted with Indonesia in his book Islam Observed
Religion: many Kurds follow this tradition as part of a tariqa network, with particular devotion to dhikr
Religion: poets Hafez and Rumi wrote love poetry within this tradition expressing desire for union with the Beloved
Religion: Mahmoud Shabestari wrote a mystical poetry collection called The Secret Rose Garden within this tradition
Religion: a saint of this tradition named Khwajah Abdullah Ansari criticized kalam theology in his work Dhamm al-Kalam
Religion: the Quran's Light Verse, describing all things as created from light, is particularly popular within this tradition
Religion: the Pakistani band Junoon exemplifies "Sufi rock," an Islam-inspired rock subgenre named for this mystical tradition
